sentences.text: To a time when I was a little boy.

I just remembered when I took the break, that not only that my father was singing to us, you know, carrying us-- because he did it with every child, must have done it with me on Shabbas or high holidays.
And it was dark.
And you have to see so many stars-- I think three stars before the Shabbas would be over.
And so he would carry us, you know.
And I remember when he carried my brother Joshua around and-- and walked back and forth and sang to him, [NON-ENGLISH].
My mother also used to sing, but she used to play the balalaika.
And she would sing.
You know, she would sing about [NON-ENGLISH] [CRYING] And-- I want to mention these things because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sorry that I didn't remember before.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that's why they will somehow may be reunited, my mother and father-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] in this hellhole.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, Auschwitz was probably the most-- one of the most terrible things that I have experienced in my life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because of the kind of beating and the kind of facilities, and you never knew when your number will be up.
You just felt like you're walking out with a verdict in your pocket without a date.
And-- but I always reflected back, when my father used to tell me to not to do this and to do that and not to-- maybe he was preparing me for these kind of things.
Because I wanted to get out of Auschwitz very badly.
So the-- every time there was a transport going someplace, I would be there, you know, to try and get on the transport.
So-- there was a transport going some place there.
And I was not supposed to be there because-- I don't remember the block of my-- they call it-- the barracks was a block-- the number of my barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I heard there's a transport going some place, so I immediately got in line.
You had to take everything off, all the clothes, and stand nude.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before-- and then there was a-- a-- usually it would be Dr.

Mengele there to tell you to-- and at the same time, they would-- and that's how they weeded people out as well, not only send them on a transport, but to send them to the gas chamber, the ones that couldn't walk too well and the ones that were getting all sick.
And so this was another after the first-- this was another, a constant weeding out.
So-- so someone said-- pointed me out that I am from block such-and-such.
And I wasn't supposed to be there.
So-- so the-- the punishment was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know that these barracks were built for horses.
They were stables.
And in the middle, in the-- in the-- they were rectangles.
So in the middle of the floor, there was like a-- to keep the horses warm, there was like a chimney that would go on the ground floor.
And you would put the fire in on one end.
And it would go out the chimney at the other end.
And on the other side was the same thing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other side was the same-- the same arrangement.
In other words, so that-- so that there was-- the fire would-- the warm smoke would go out both ends.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Well, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I was not supposed to be there, the punishment was that I get whipped.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So there were two-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two-- I think they were Cossacks-- prisoners-- Russian prisoners.
And they were-- and they stuck my head in there.
There was no fire in it.
It was daytime.
We never had a fire there, anyways.
I never remembered that there was ever a fire in these.
It was only when the horses were in these stables.
So-- and they told me to count.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and every time I count, they were hitting me from both sides.
I think they were like-- it looked to me like it was like a twisted cable.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Then I passed out.
I don't how-- after know how many-- how long I counted.
Like, it was very, very sick.
And I had no skin on my rear, because I had like, open wounds.
And I was concerned about infection and-- and how am I going to heal?
I wouldn't dare go to the infirmary, because that would be the end.
So friends would help me along.
And I somehow, you know, being young guy, I-- I recovered from that.
And-- but I didn't want to do anything to go to another transport, because I wasn't completely healed yet.
And I remember one-- one morning, we were out on the report platz, which is where they count, and everything took place on this platz.
And-- and we were kept there for quite a while.
And there was-- the rumor had it that Eichmann, you know, is coming to inspect the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] his troops, you know, his sacrificial lambs.
And he came.
So we were there for quite a while.
Many people just couldn't stand any-- couldn't take it.
They just keeled over.
And a lot of people died that day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Well, I-- I gradually felt better.
And I-- Did you see Eichmann that day?
I-- I-- yes, I saw him that day.
And what happened?
Did he walk around to everyone?
No.
He-- we would never come close to us.
Because he would-- he didn't want you-- have you-- lice and all the diseases and all of the-- they were very-- you know, they were-- that's why they wouldn't touch you with their hand.
They always used the rifle butt or-- whatever they had, a stick or a whip.
I think so.
There was a-- a whole entourage of SS troopers.
And-- and I-- I think that I-- I saw him.
But I can't-- among them, I'm sure.
Because the rumor had it that Eichmann is-- you know.
And you were not allowed to talk in the ranks.
But they were whispering anyways, because they wouldn't get-- get near you.
They were only counting in front or looking at the side and behind you, but never were inside the ranks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if they wanted to send someone in, they would usually send in the kapo to get you out or whatever punishment you should receive.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I was starting to feel better, and I-- and there was a transport going to the Krupps factory.
And I-- also was not my barracks, but-- so I went to-- got undressed, I went and got into the line, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I saw Mengele.
Maybe he was standing a little bit behind you, you know?
And he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
It means turn around, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And he says-- he didn't say anything.
And he sent me where all these old people went and the ones that couldn't walk.
And so I realized that I'm-- I'm doomed.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but I never put my clothes back on.
And I somehow maneuvered to get back with the people that were standing in the nude.
Because the other people, they said, you can get dressed, the ones that-- there were two selections made always-- the ones that were sent on a transport and the ones sent to the gas chamber.
At least this is the experience that I-- and that's how I realized what was happening when I saw the sick and the lame and the old.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I came closer-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- you had to stay in the line.
And the lines get pretty long.
You know, they take a few hundred people.

And so-- and they walked-- they called Mengele for something-- I don't know-- for a phone call or some-- in other words, he told his assistant, was a-- like Mengele must have been like maybe a Hauptsturmfuhrer, and the other guy must have been a Sturmfuhrer.
So he called him, and he said to take over.
And I go-- and they said to me-- in the line, says-- when I got in the line, the guy's standing there and say, remember, when he asks you [SPEAKING GERMAN], tell him, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- metalworks.
Because the rumor had it that this transport is going to the Krupps factory, to Funfteichen.
At that time, we didn't know-- we didn't know the name of Funfteichen.
But that's what really happened.
So when I got back, and this young man, this Sturmfuhrer says, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And so I turned around, and he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
So he sent me to the right.
And that's how I got out of Auschwitz.
I went to Funfteichen.
I want to ask you a couple things about Auschwitz, OK?
Since that's what we're talking about, before you get to the Krupps factory.
First, I know that you said that you knew that people died in Auschwitz.
When you were-- but how did you really-- did you know they were-- when did you know that people were gassed and about the crematoriums?
When--
All this I have learned from my father.
Before coming to Auschwitz-- right.
There was always the-- the smell of-- like burnt rubber.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I realized after a while, you know, that-- and everybody else was talking about it.
The crematoriums have been fired up again-- in other words, because they were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Auschwitz, they were going to day and night, the crematoriums.
So there was a constant smell.

And there was also-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a film of-- not soot, but you could see sometimes that there is something that created like a film on the grass or-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or maybe on the concrete, where-- so you knew exactly what-- where it comes from.
When-- when they took my father away, I was totally devastated.
And I couldn't stop crying-- same thing with my brother when I didn't see him anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
No.
They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all I remember is what my father told me to-- you have to be strong in order to survive, no matter what happens.
And I followed his advice.
Naturally, I mourned, and I was crying.
And I felt like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like the most precious possession was taken from me, especially when I was-- this was my last link with my father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But then I became very bitter and very mad.
And I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I always-- since then, I always thought, when the time will come and I see them fall, I'm going to take revenge.
I have to stay alive, because I'm going to go out-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with two machine guns in each-- one machine gun-- and mow all the Germans that I see down.
Many people did that, you know.
But this was then, when I was-- when I was-- you start thinking differently when you become a human being again.
This is the kind of thinking I was doing when I was reduced to below the grade of an animal.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And-- and because of his advice to me and all the things that he-- that he taught me, I probably am-- that's why I am alive now.
Because I was always cognizant of things, that something will happen, and something bad will happen.
Because you could never expect anything good to happen.
But I have to overcome.
Whatever it comes my way, I have to rise above it.
Because I want to see them pay for what they have done to my family, to my-- to my-- especially to my mother and father and my two brothers and-- and the rest of the family.
But I always thought of-- that I am going to take revenge.
There's no question about it.
And if this would have been the time for me to be free, that's what I probably would have done.
And-- but you start living again as a human.
And your thoughts are a little different.
I'm-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was very happy.
I couldn't say I was-- I was grateful that I-- that I was able to leave Auschwitz.
And my perspective has changed somewhat, you know- that you're going to a-- to a concentration camp that is more like a forced labor camp, in my own mind.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And maybe conditions will be different.
But really, they were not.
By-- by cattle car.
We were loaded up at Auschwitz, and we went by car to-- I think we probably arrived like the next day.
And--
Well, they were the same conditions, but not the same atmosphere, because you didn't see the pregnant women and the old people and the babies.
And you-- you didn't think of it the same way that, you know-- you didn't think of death.
You thought of life, because you were going to be working, you know?
And I realized that-- my father always told me you have to be strong in order to work.
So-- because if you work, you will stay alive, he told me, you see.
And so I-- I lived by that.
And-- and they were a little-- the conditions were a little different.
It they were not-- at least there were not 10 people into a-- a-- a-- in a row of-- of-- bunks, you know that that-- in Auschwitz, I don't remember if they even put straws.
They never-- we never had a mattress.
It was-- maybe there was some straw.
I don't remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But Funfteichen was already a little different.
They-- you-- the bunks were next to each other, but it was separate bunks.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] first morning we got up-- first of all, the barracks were not ready.
So we all slept on the floor the first night.
Because we helped to finish the camp.
We built a concentration camp in Funfteichen.
And they brought people from Auschwitz and people from another forced labor camp, which was called Markstedt.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And that's how we-- and so the barracks were not ready, so we were-- we were told to clean the barracks.
I said, where is the broom?
So they said, the broom is on your hat.
In other words, he says, you stupid jerk.
They give us like a round hat, you know, with the stripes.
And we used our hat to sweep the barracks out and to erect the bunks.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the next day, they assigned us to work.
And they called the arbeitskommandos, which was a little bit more orderly already.
Most people were assigned to-- to-- the Krupps factory.
And I was not that lucky.
I was assigned to work on-- build bridges, lay railroad tracks, dig ditches for sewer lines and-- and I worked for a company, Henckel and Zeiner, Henckel and Sons.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now they are very well known by their knives they manufacture, Henckel knives.
But they were-- at that time, that's what we were doing.
And many people died there, too, from the exposure.
Because we worked winter time.
It was already getting cold.
And we-- and so it was very, very cold, and we were wearing those pajama uniforms-- very thin.
And the only thing, really, that kept people alive, is maybe some were a little stronger.
I was younger.
Yes, constantly.
We had a kapo that was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a heavy-set guy, you know.
I understand that he was a furniture mover, you know.
He used to-- they called him the Schlepper, because they used to move furniture from one place to another without putting it on a truck.
They would just move furniture all day long, would take a whole mattress and bath and whole-- and tie it up and schlep it over to a few-- maybe a half a mile away or so.
And that-- yeah.
So-- and he was a pretty rough guy.
And he was probably-- he worked very hard all his life, and he was probably very disappointed with society.
So he was getting even, you know, with it.
And he was rough.
Many people, from the cold weather, from exposure to the cold, they were frost-bitten, and they were-- got sick.
And many-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where I was, you know, because in the-- in the Krupps factory, you worked inside under a roof.
At least there was-- you were doing-- unless you're loading or unloading, but most of the time, you worked inside.
But I was always exposed to the elements.
If it was raining, we were soaking wet.
If it was snowing, we-- it was freezing.
So people were getting-- I-- I noticed the same situation as in Auschwitz.
They were getting-- first their feet would swell, and then their head would swell, and then they would just keel over.
And--
The food was-- we were given already a little-- a little more decent.
But it was-- it seemed like there was like-- we were given a piece of bread in the morning.
And at night, we were given a bowl of soup.
The soup was-- you could taste the sand, in other words.
It looked like-- seemed like they-- they never cooked it.
They-- had potatoes that were not peeled and cabbage and whatever they threw in there.
And-- but at least they had a place where you can sit down on benches and-- and a table.
And-- and you can at least eat in a-- in a sub-human way, but a little more improved than Auschwitz.
Sanitary conditions, they were always concerned, because they were already getting more closer, you see, with the kind of work, and also where the people were going to the Krupps factory.
So they were-- they had better sanitary conditions, where you can-- at least you could-- but everything was like a mass production.
There were holes through-- the same kind of a concept.
But at least you can take a shower.
I-- I never remember getting a-- ever getting another uniform.
I mean-- so maybe-- I don't know-- but at least you could take a shower.
A constant.
That was a constant process of delousing.
But I would not even-- many times I would-- I would take my-- I would strip, and it was under freezing conditions.
Because they had showers on the outside, you know.
So the water became ice-- icicles.
But at least I was able to spread my shirt and take off my trousers and spread it out so that the lice would freeze.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and so this also helped.
Because you were eaten alive from-- from the bugs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then what happened to you next?
And I kept working on the same-- but we had a-- a-- a lagerfuhrer.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe he was the reportfuhrer.
His name was Schamal.
He was a sadist, a murderer.
